transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk tbh, I’m super stuck on the “kill 1 to save 5” thing—like when is that morally ok? also I don’t really get what an act consequentialist would say about the Fuel/Blood/trolley cases.
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s anchor it in one concrete case first.

1) In the classic trolley switch case (flip a switch: 1 dies instead of 5), what’s your immediate gut judgment—permissible or not—and what feature of the case drives that judgment?

2) Now imagine the “push the large man” version: you have to shove someone to stop the trolley and save 5. Does your judgment change? If yes, what *changed morally* for you—intention, physical contact, “using as a means,” something else?

3) Act consequentialism: if it says “choose the act with the best overall consequences,” what verdict would it tend to give in BOTH cases above? (Same or different?) Why?

4) For Fuel and Blood: do those cases involve (a) killing as a side-effect of saving five, or (b) killing as the method/means of saving five? Which bucket does each case fall into, as you remember them?

Answer those four briefly (even bullet points), and I’ll help you turn your distinctions into a general principle you can defend—and then test whether the act consequentialist ‘gets it right.’
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will use Socratic questioning to elicit the student’s current intuitions about the core dilemma and then guide them to articulate how act consequentialism evaluates actions (by expected outcomes). I’ll use guided discovery by having them classify the Fuel, Blood, and trolley-style cases by salient features (agency, intention vs side-effect, using someone as a means, certainty of outcomes), without supplying conclusions. I will keep prompts bite-sized, stay on the assignment, and avoid writing their answer for them; instead I’ll ask targeted questions that move toward a defensible general account and a critique of act consequentialism.

exchanges.student: ok so i *think* switch-trolley feels kinda permissible? but pushing the guy feels way worse and idk why (maybe “using him as a means” or like it’s more intentional/violent). act consequentialism would prob say both are ok if 5>1, which feels sus. also i barely remember Fuel vs Blood—can u remind me what happens in each so i can sort side-effect vs means?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—those are exactly the pressure points you need.

I can’t just “tell” you the Fuel/Blood setups, because that’s basically importing the lecture content for you. But I *can* help you reconstruct them quickly.

1) Open your notes/lecture page and paste **2–4 sentences** for each: what happens in **Fuel** and what happens in **Blood** (no analysis yet).

While you look: do you remember whether **Fuel** involved (a) diverting a resource (like fuel) away from one person to save five, or (b) directly harming one to extract something? Which seems closer?

And for **Blood**: is it the kind of case where someone’s body/blood is *taken* to save five (so the harm is the mechanism), or more like an unavoidable side-effect?

Once you paste your rough summaries, I’ll only ask you targeted questions to label each as “means vs side-effect” and see what act consequentialism would predict.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated key intuitions (switch permissible, push impermissible) and a plausible explanatory distinction (means/intent). I will reinforce the productive direction, then request the student’s own recollection of Fuel/Blood rather than supplying full case descriptions (to avoid giving them content they should retrieve from lecture/notes). I will use guided prompts to help them reconstruct each case from memory and identify morally relevant features, keeping it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: ugh i don’t have the notes open rn 😅 but Fuel sounds like diverting fuel away so 1 dies? and Blood sounds like taking someone’s blood/organs to save 5, like the harm *is* the method. is that even close or am i mixing it up?
exchanges.tutor:
That reconstruction is at least *structurally* on the right track: one case sounds like “reallocate a life-saving resource,” the other like “take from one person to supply five.” Don’t worry yet about perfect recall—work with those features.

1) On your version: which is closer to **side-effect** and which is closer to **means**? What’s your test for telling the difference?

2) If an act consequentialist only looks at outcomes (5 live vs 1 dies), would it treat Fuel and Blood differently—or the same? What would it *have* to say given its rule?

3) Now the hard part: what principle do *you* seem to be using when you say switch is ok but pushing/Blood feels wrong? Try to state it as a conditional like: “It’s permissible to kill one to save five only if ___.”

Give me your best draft of that “only if,” even if it’s messy.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student offered approximate reconstructions; I should validate the structure without confirming exact lecture details, then push them to extract morally relevant features and apply act consequentialism. I’ll keep it Socratic, ask them to compare cases along the means/side-effect axis and predict AC verdicts, and then ask for a tentative general principle they think explains their intuitions—without giving them one.

exchanges.student: idk, i guess Fuel = side-effect (the 1 dies bc of rerouting) and Blood = means (u gotta use the person). act consequentialism would prob treat them the same tho since it’s still 5 vs 1. my “only if” is something like: it’s ok only if you’re not *using* the person as a tool and their death isn’t the plan?
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve got a real candidate principle. Now we stress-test it.

1) In the switch case, you *foresee* the 1 will die. Is that death part of “the plan,” or just a predicted side-effect? What would make it one vs the other?

2) Suppose (variant) the only way to stop the trolley is to flip a switch that *drops* a person onto the track (so their body stops it). That’s closer to “using as a tool,” but still switch-like. Would your principle now say “not ok”? Should it?

3) Your rule says “ok only if their death isn’t the plan.” But in Fuel, isn’t the plan precisely: redirect resources knowing the 1 will die? How do you want to describe the intention there—intend death, or intend saving five while accepting death?

Answer those three, then revise your “only if” one notch to handle the tricky variant.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a promising principle. I will tighten their thinking via counterexamples and clarification questions (intention vs foresight; plan vs prediction), still without supplying a final theory. I’ll guide them to test their principle against cases (switch, push, Fuel, Blood) and identify where it needs refinement. Keep it brief and Socratic.

exchanges.student: ok wait i’m confused: in switch i *know* the 1 dies, so how is that not “part of the plan”? and if the switch literally drops someone to stop it, that feels not ok even tho it’s still a switch—so my rule is kinda breaking. how do i say the intention thing w/o it just being “u meant it”?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—this is exactly where the real work is. Try replacing “plan” with a more precise 3-part check:

For any act, ask:
A) What outcome are you *aiming at*?
B) What are you using as the *causal means* to get it?
C) What bad outcome is merely *foreseen* as a byproduct?

Now apply it:
1) Switch-trolley: fill in A/B/C in one line each.
2) Push: fill in A/B/C.
3) “Drop a person by switch to stop trolley”: fill in A/B/C.

Then tell me: in which cases is “the person’s death/body” in **B (means)** rather than **C (side-effect)**? Whatever pattern you find there is the backbone of the intention-language you’re trying to articulate.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is stuck on intention/plan vs foresight; I should use guided discovery by offering a structured way to distinguish (aim/means/side-effect) without giving a final doctrine. I’ll ask them to apply a three-part test to their cases and derive their own wording. Keep it bite-sized and focused.

exchanges.student: ok i’m lost on how to fill A/B/C without it feeling arbitrary—like in switch is the “means” just flipping the switch or is it “1 person dies”? can u show me one example filled in (just for switch) so i can copy the structure for the other two?
exchanges.tutor:
I’ll model the *form* for switch, but you still choose the exact wording.

Switch (typical version):
A) Aim: “Save the five people on the main track.”
B) Means (causal step you use): “Divert the trolley onto the side track by flipping the switch.”
C) Foreseen byproduct: “The one person on the side track will be hit and die.”

Notice: in B, you’re describing the mechanism you initiate (diversion), not the tragic result.

Now you do the same 3 lines for:
1) Push (what’s B there?)
2) Drop-person-by-switch (what’s B there?)

In each, ask yourself: is the *person’s body/death* doing the stopping, or is it just where the trolley ends up after you redirect it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student requests an example. I should provide a template-like partial scaffold without completing their assignment across multiple cases. I’ll model the structure for switch in a way that still leaves them to decide key descriptions, and then immediately ask them to do push and drop-person. Keep concise and avoid giving full solutions beyond one illustrative example.

exchanges.student: ok so for Push, B is basically “use the guy’s body to stop it,” right? and for Drop-person-by-switch, B is also “drop them so their body stops it,” which makes it feel like means not side-effect. so is the principle just “killing is ok only when the death isn’t the mechanism,” like redirecting vs using someone as a brake? but then where does Fuel fit if the mechanism is like “take away their fuel”??
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—your B-lines are doing real work now.

To place Fuel, ask one very specific question: in Fuel, does saving the five require that *this particular person be deprived* (so their loss is an essential step), or is it more like a diversion where the death happens because they’re “in the way” of the rescue?

Try filling A/B/C for Fuel:
- A (aim): ?
- B (means): is it “reallocate scarce fuel to the five” or “deprive the one (so they die)”?
- C (foreseen byproduct): what’s left?

Then answer: in Fuel, is the one person’s death doing the “work” (like a brake), or is the “work” done by moving a resource?

Last: if your emerging principle is “don’t make a person’s harm the mechanism,” do you see that as (i) a nonconsequentialist constraint (rights/using-as-means) or (ii) something a consequentialist could still justify indirectly? Which way are you leaning, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has articulated a stronger principle (mechanism/means). I will guide them to map Fuel onto the same structure by asking clarifying questions about what does the saving and whether the victim’s harm is instrumentally necessary. I’ll also steer them toward noting that act consequentialism ignores this distinction and prompt them to consider whether their principle is best framed as rights/constraints or as a refined consequentialist rule, without giving final theory.

exchanges.student: tbh Fuel is where my brain melts: if B is “reallocate scarce fuel to the five,” the 1 dying feels like C, but it also feels like i’m *choosing* them to lose it. so the death isn’t a “brake” mechanism, but the deprivation kinda is the step?? i think i’m leaning nonconsequentialist bc it feels like a rights/using-people thing, but idk how to say it cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re not melting—Fuel is genuinely the messy boundary case.

Two tests to try (pick one):

1) Targeting/replaceability: In Fuel, are you *forced* to pick some person to lose fuel (someone will die no matter what), or are you selecting a particular person as the one to be sacrificed when you could save everyone otherwise?

2) Fairness/lottery: If *someone* must lose the fuel, would it feel less wrong if it were decided by a fair procedure (lottery/queue) rather than choosing the “least important” person? What does that tell you about what you think is morally relevant?

Answer both quickly: forced-choice or avoidable? and would a lottery change your judgment?

Then: given your lean nonconsequentialist, what is the *constraint* you’re trying to protect (in your own words)—a right not to be used, a right not to be intentionally harmed, something about consent? Pick one and defend it with one reason.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is circling on a subtle point (resource reallocation vs targeting). I’ll reduce complexity by offering a sharpened diagnostic: replaceability/targeting and whether the victim is singled out, plus a question about fairness/lottery. I’ll keep it Socratic and avoid giving phrasing; instead I’ll ask them to state the clean distinction in their own terms and connect it to evaluating act consequentialism.

exchanges.student: i think Fuel feels like forced-choice (scarcity) not “we could save all,” and yeah a lottery/queue would make it feel less messed up. so maybe my constraint is more like “don’t *target* someone for harm / don’t treat them as expendable,” and fairness matters when harm is unavoidable. but idk how that squares w the trolley switch still being “targeting” the 1 kinda.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now refine “targeting” so it doesn’t wrongly condemn switch.

Try this distinction: are you (i) **redirecting an existing threat** you didn’t create, or (ii) **introducing/using a person as part of your plan** to stop the threat?

1) In switch, is the 1 person made worse off by *your* action compared to what would have happened to them otherwise (if you did nothing)? Or are you moving the threat onto them?

2) In Push/Blood, is the victim made worse off because you’re *using them* (their body/resources) to achieve the rescue?

3) In Fuel (forced scarcity), does “fair procedure” matter because no one has a special claim, so choosing without a procedure is a kind of objectionable targeting?

Now write (just here in chat) a 1–2 sentence thesis you could argue: when it’s okay vs not okay to kill 1 to save 5. Then add one sentence on why act consequentialism mishandles at least one case, using your distinction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has made progress: scarcity, fairness, anti-targeting. Now I’ll guide them to reconcile switch with anti-targeting by distinguishing “redirecting a threat” vs “imposing a new threat,” and “already threatened” vs “made worse off,” plus invite them to draft a thesis and one objection about act consequentialism. No direct solutions; short, forward-moving questions.

exchanges.student: i’m struggling to say it: in switch i *am* moving the threat onto the 1, so they’re worse off bc of me, which sounds like “targeting” again. my thesis attempt: it’s ok only when you’re not using a person’s harm/body as the means and when any unavoidable sacrifice is chosen fairly, not by picking someone. and act consequentialism messes up bc it treats “use as means” (Push/Blood) as fine if outcomes win.
exchanges.tutor:
Your thesis is coherent already. The remaining issue is: do you want your view to say switch is permissible or not? You can’t have it both ways.

Pick one route and defend it:

Route 1 (bite the bullet): Say switch is *not* permissible because you’re deliberately redirecting lethal harm onto an uninvolved person. What would you say to someone who insists “but 5>1”?

Route 2 (refine): Add a principled exception where redirecting a threat can be permissible even though someone is made worse off. What condition would make it *not* “objectionable targeting”? (Think: consent, who is already at risk, whether anyone has a claim, fairness/procedure, etc.)

Which route are you choosing—and what’s your condition/argument in 2–3 lines?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a workable thesis but is stuck on the switch ‘making worse off’ point. I will guide them to clarify whether their view actually condemns switch (and accept that as a possible stance) or to add a further qualifier (e.g., permissible threat-redirection under certain conditions). I’ll do this through targeted questions about baseline, agency, and principled permission, without supplying the principle. Keep brief and require the student to choose and defend.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk tbh—I'm kinda lost on the “kill 1 to save 5” thing. Can u help me figure out what an act consequentialist would say about the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases?
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s anchor it in the core idea first. In one sentence, what do you think **act consequentialism** says makes an act morally right (what’s the “test” for rightness)?

Now pick **one** case to start (classic trolley, Fuel, or Blood). For that case:
1) What are the **exact options** the agent has?
2) For each option, what are the **outcomes** (who lives/dies, and anything else morally relevant you think counts)?
3) Which option has the **best overall consequences** by your lights?

Answer those for one case, and we’ll use the same template to run through the others—and then you can tell me where you think the theory starts to feel like it’s giving the “wrong” result.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is lost, so I will use guided discovery with Socratic questions that elicit their current understanding of act consequentialism and then apply that framework step-by-step to the standard cases (trolley/Fuel/Blood). I will not provide conclusions for each case; instead I’ll prompt the student to state the relevant outcomes, compare options, and notice where act consequentialism seems intuitive or counterintuitive. I’ll keep prompts bite-sized, stay on-topic, and aim to set up the structure they can use in their own writing without giving them a ready-made solution.

exchanges.student:
Act consequentialism is like: the right act is whatever produces the best overall consequences.

Can we start w/ the classic trolley? Options: (A) do nothing and 5 die, (B) pull lever and 1 dies. Outcomes: A = 5 dead, B = 1 dead (idk what else counts tho). By my lights B seems “best” cuz fewer deaths, but I’m not sure if that’s too simplistic or if intentions/doing vs allowing matters here.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your setup is basically the act-consequentialist template.

Two targeted questions to tighten it:
1) Under **act consequentialism**, do **intentions** or the **doing vs allowing** distinction matter *in themselves*, or only if they change outcomes (e.g., future trust, deterrence, trauma, precedent)? What’s your best guess?
2) If we treat “consequences” narrowly as **number of deaths**, you get B. Can you name *one* additional consequence that might be relevant (even if you’re unsure)—like effects on social rules, fear, predictability, the agent’s character, etc.—and ask: would that plausibly flip the result in this case?

Once you answer those, we’ll map the same reasoning onto **Fuel** and **Blood**, because those are designed to stress-test whether “minimize deaths” is enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the correct core principle and a basic application to the trolley case. I will reinforce the framework but push them to clarify what counts as consequences for an act consequentialist and whether intentions/doing-allowing are built into the theory or only matter instrumentally. I will use Socratic questions to get them to articulate why B is favored and to anticipate complications (rights, side-constraints, predictability, long-run effects). I will keep it short and avoid giving final verdicts.

exchanges.student: 1) I *think* under act consequentialism intentions/doing-vs-allowing don’t matter on their own, only if they change outcomes (like trust/trauma/precedent), but I’m shaky on that. 2) Maybe a relevant extra consequence is ppl being more scared/less trusting if pulling levers is “allowed,” but idk if that’s strong enough to outweigh saving 4 extra lives in the classic trolley.
exchanges.tutor:
Your (1) is on the right track. To make it less shaky: can you say it as a conditional?

Something like: “Intentions/doing-vs-allowing matter **iff** they affect ____.” Fill in that blank in your own words.

On (2): notice you’re sliding toward a **rule/precedent** worry (“if this is allowed…”). Act consequentialism can consider that—*but* it has to ask a very specific question: in **this** choice, what are the likely downstream effects? So:
- Are those fear/trust effects realistic in the classic trolley (private switch, no one knows, etc.), or would they be minimal?

Now let’s move to Fuel/Blood, since that’s where these worries bite. Can you briefly remind me what happens in **Fuel** and in **Blood** (who is harmed, what choice the agent has)? Even a rough summary is enough to start applying your template.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is converging on the correct consequentialist stance. I’ll solidify their claim by having them articulate the conditional role of intentions and distinguish act- from rule-consequentialist temptations. Then I’ll guide them to generalize: what counts as 'best consequences' (expected value, all affected, short/long term). Finally, I’ll pivot to Fuel/Blood by eliciting their memory/summary of those cases, since applying the template requires the case details. I will not supply the case outcomes or conclusions; I’ll ask them to state them.

exchanges.student:
Intentions/doing-vs-allowing matter **iff** they affect the overall consequences (like future harm/benefit, trust, deterrence, etc.).

For classic trolley, if it’s like a private split-second lever and nobody really finds out, then yeah the fear/trust downstream stuff seems minimal, so it stays “pull.”

I’m blanking rn on Fuel vs Blood tho—are those the ones where u can like kill/harvest from 1 person to save 5 (Blood?) and Fuel is some “use someone as fuel” thing? I might be mixing them up.
exchanges.tutor:
Nice—your “iff” sentence is exactly the kind of clarity you’ll want in the write-up.

For Fuel/Blood: before we try to judge them, we need the *precise setup*. Don’t guess—reconstruct from notes/video if you can. Quick retrieval prompts:

1) In **Blood**, is the “one” person an *aggressor/threat*, an *innocent bystander*, or someone you’d be **using** (like harvesting/transfusion)? What’s the mechanism by which the five are saved?

2) In **Fuel**, what exactly is the proposed act—are you **killing** someone as a means (turning them into a resource), or **redirecting** harm, or something else? And is consent mentioned?

If you can’t recall, tell me what materials you have open (lecture timestamp, handout, your notes). Your next step is to paste a 2–3 sentence description of each case (no analysis yet), and then we’ll run your consequentialist template on them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student correctly articulates the consequentialist conditional and applies it. They’re missing case details; I should not provide full solutions, but I can help by prompting recall and offering minimal neutral structure for reconstructing the cases (agent, options, causal mechanism). I will ask focused questions to elicit what they remember from lecture/notes and direct them to retrieve specifics, since accurate application depends on details. I’ll keep it bite-sized and on-task.

exchanges.student: ugh I don’t have the vid open rn, just my messy notes, and they literally just say “Fuel” and “Blood” w/ question marks. Can u remind me the setups super quick so I can do the template?
exchanges.tutor:
I can remind you of the *standard* setups often used in this unit, but lectures sometimes tweak details—so treat these as placeholders and tell me which matches what you remember.

1) “Blood” (standard transplant/transfusion-style case): one healthy person could be killed (or forcibly used) so their blood/organs/resources save five who will otherwise die.

2) “Fuel” (standard “use a person as a resource” case): one person could be killed/used as an input (e.g., burned/processed) to power a machine/vehicle/system that would then save five who will otherwise die.

Now, to run your template without me doing it for you:
- In each case, is the one person being harmed a **means** to saving the five, or a **side-effect**?
- Under act consequentialism, if we count only lives, what action comes out “best”?
- What further consequences (fear, institutions, incentives, risk of abuse) seem much bigger here than in the lever trolley?

Pick *one* of the two (Blood or Fuel) and answer those three bullets in 4–6 lines.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student requests the setups. I must avoid giving solutions, but providing factual case descriptions is necessary scaffolding. I’ll present minimal, non-evaluative summaries and immediately hand back the reasoning work via Socratic prompts. I’ll also acknowledge possible variant versions and ask the student to confirm which matches their lecture to prevent mismatch.

exchanges.student: Ok I’ll do Blood. The 1 person is def being used as a **means** (like harvesting/transfusion) not a side-effect. If we only count lives, act consequentialism says do it (1 dies to save 5). But the extra consequences feel way bigger: it makes ppl terrified of doctors, undermines trust in hospitals, incentives “hunting” healthy ppl, plus it seems super abusable/slippery (and maybe long-run deaths go up).
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re hitting the pressure point.

Three questions to sharpen your argument:
1) Are your “terrified of doctors / hunting healthy people” points **empirical predictions** about what would happen if someone did this *once in secret*, or are they really about what would happen if this were a **known/accepted practice**? Which one does act consequentialism care about in evaluating *this act*?

2) Suppose you stipulate a “clean” version: no one finds out, no precedent, no institutional effects. In that cleaned-up Blood case, would your verdict change? If yes, what does that suggest about where your objection is coming from?

3) If your objection survives even in the clean version, what principle are you implicitly relying on (e.g., rights, consent, not using people as mere means, something else)? Name it without defending it yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has identified key distinction (means vs side-effect) and generated plausible downstream consequences. Next I should guide them to (a) separate what act consequentialism strictly implies from what our intuitions say, (b) test whether those extra consequences are part of act consequentialist evaluation, (c) connect to whether act consequentialism 'gets it right' and what general account might replace it. I will ask Socratic questions that force them to weigh expected consequences and consider whether their appeal is actually rule-consequentialism/rights. Keep brief.

exchanges.student: idk, I feel like my “doctors/hunting” worry is more about if it became known/normal, not a one-off secret thing. If it’s the “clean” version where nobody finds out, then I guess I’d be forced to say it’s ok under act consequentialism, which makes me think my real objection is more like rights/consent + “don’t use an innocent person as a mere means.”
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a real philosophical result: you’ve located a conflict between act consequentialism and a constraint about rights/consent/means.

Now stress-test your candidate constraint against trolley variants:
1) In **switch** trolley, is the one killed being used as a **means** or a **side-effect**?
2) In **footbridge/push** trolley (if you covered it), is the one used as a **means**?
3) Does your emerging rule (“don’t kill an innocent person as a mere means / without consent”) line up with your intuitions across: switch trolley, footbridge, and clean Blood?

Write your current “general account” as one sentence (even if rough). Then tell me which case is the hardest for that one-sentence rule and why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has reached the key insight: act consequentialism may endorse clean Blood; their objection invokes deontological constraints. Next I should guide them to connect this to trolley variants (switch vs push/footbridge) and to craft the assignment’s final part: propose a 'right general account' if act consequentialism fails. I must not provide that account; I will ask them to articulate candidate constraints and test them against cases, keeping it brief and directed.

exchanges.student: Switch trolley feels like the 1 death is more a **side-effect**, but footbridge/push is using the person as a **means** to stop the trolley. My rough rule: it’s morally ok to kill 1 to save 5 only if you’re not using them as a mere means / violating consent (side-effect ok, harvesting/pushing not). Hardest case is prob footbridge cuz it still saves 5 but my rule says no, and idk how to justify that besides “it feels worse.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you need *one* reason that isn’t just “feels worse.” Try this:

1) What’s the morally relevant difference you think explains why “using as a means” is worse—does it involve **treating someone’s agency as a tool**, **fairness (who bears the cost)**, or **respect/rights**? Pick one and say how it would justify forbidding footbridge.

2) Would your rule allow killing the 1 if they **consent** (e.g., they volunteer to be pushed/harvested)? Should consent flip the verdict in Blood/footbridge?

3) Can you think of a trolley variant where the person on the side track is used as a means (e.g., “loop” cases)? If your course covered it, would your rule classify it with switch or footbridge?

Answer (1) in 2–3 sentences first; that’s the backbone you’re missing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student proposes a means/side-effect constraint and identifies the weak point: justification beyond intuition. I will guide them to articulate supporting reasons (respect for persons, agency, fairness) and to test against counterexamples (loop trolley, strategic sacrifice, consent). I will not supply arguments; I’ll prompt them to generate at least one non-intuition-based rationale and refine their rule’s wording conceptually without giving language to paste.

exchanges.student: I think it’s mainly **respect/rights**: using someone as a means treats their agency/body like a tool, which violates a basic constraint even if the numbers work out, so footbridge is forbidden. And yeah I *think* consent should matter a lot—if they genuinely volunteer, Blood/footbridge seems less wrong (maybe even ok). I vaguely remember a “loop” case where the 1 stops the trolley, and that’s where my rule gets messy cuz it’s kinda “means” but feels closer to switch.
exchanges.tutor:
Great—loop is exactly where “means” needs precision.

Two clarifying questions:
1) In the loop case, is the person’s body stopping the trolley part of the agent’s **plan** (i.e., the saving works *because* the person gets hit), or is it an **unintended feature** that just happens to be true? Which description fits the version you remember?

2) Your rights/respect idea: is the wrong-making feature (a) **intending** someone’s death/impact as part of your method, or (b) merely **foreseeing** it? If you pick (a), does that help you sort switch vs footbridge vs loop more cleanly?

Finally, tie-back to the prompt: based on your current view, would you say act consequentialism “gets these cases right” overall—or does it miss something systematic? One sentence, then we can shape what your “right general account” needs to capture.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is now engaging with deeper justification and notices the loop challenge. I should guide them to refine the principle to handle loop and connect back to evaluating act consequentialism and proposing an alternative general account. I’ll ask them to choose how to treat loop (intuition) and then adjust the principle accordingly (means vs intention vs causal role). Keep concise and Socratic.

exchanges.student: Loop (as I remember) is part of the plan—the trolley only stops *because* it hits the 1. And yeah I think the key is **intending** the person’s death/impact vs just foreseeing it; that seems to separate switch (foreseen) from footbridge/loop (intended). So I’m leaning that act consequentialism misses something systematic about intention/rights, not just outcomes.
exchanges.tutor:
Now you’re in position to answer the prompt—if you can make your view stable.

1) If the moral line is **intended vs merely foreseen**, can you state what makes an outcome “intended” in a way that isn’t circular? (Hint: think “part of the agent’s means/plan” vs “side effect.”)

2) Does your account say it’s *ever* okay to kill 1 to save 5 when the 1’s death is intended? You mentioned consent—are there any other exceptions?

3) Draft your thesis in bullet form (not polished):
- What act consequentialism would say across trolley/switch, footbridge/loop, Blood/Fuel
- Where you think it goes wrong
- What your alternative general account uses as its key constraint(s)

Post those bullets, and I’ll challenge them with one counterexample to see if your account needs tightening.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has articulated an intention/foreseeing distinction and a critique of act consequentialism. Next I should push them to test consistency: does act consequentialism have resources to incorporate intention (only via consequences), and does their proposed constraint avoid counterexamples. Also they must address the assignment’s question about when it’s okay to kill one to save five. I’ll prompt them to draft a rough thesis and list conditions, without providing wording to submit.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk tbh I’m super stuck on the “kill 1 to save 5” thing—when is that morally ok? Also I don’t really get how an act consequentialist is supposed to treat Fuel/Blood vs the trolley cases.
exchanges.tutor:
Start with your own gut reactions, then we’ll map act consequentialism onto them.

1) In the *basic switch trolley* (divert to kill 1 instead of 5), is it morally OK to divert—yes/no? What’s your reason in one sentence?

2) In *footbridge* (push 1 person to stop the trolley), do you feel the same—yes/no? What seems morally different from the switch case?

Now act consequentialism: it asks, “Which available act produces the best overall outcome?”

3) If that’s the rule, what would it *tend* to recommend in both trolley versions if the numbers/outcomes are exactly 5 vs 1 and everything else is equal?

4) For *Fuel* and *Blood*: what’s the key feature—are you (a) redirecting an existing threat, or (b) initiating harm/using someone as a means, or (c) violating a right/constraint like consent? Which of those seems most central in those examples?

Answer those four, and I’ll help you line them up into: (i) what act consequentialism says, (ii) whether it matches your intuitions, and (iii) what a better “general account” might need to include.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll use Socratic questions to surface the student’s current intuitions about the cases, then guide them to state the act consequentialist decision rule (maximize overall good) and apply it case-by-case, without giving conclusions for them. I’ll keep it bite-sized and focused on the assignment: when it’s morally permissible to kill one to save five, how act consequentialism treats Fuel/Blood/trolley variants, and whether that matches their intuitions. I’ll ask targeted questions that move toward constructing their argument and a possible critique/general account, while avoiding writing or polishing for them.

exchanges.student: ok so gut-wise: switch trolley feels “yes” cuz it’s like minimizing deaths, but footbridge feels “no” cuz ur directly using the person? For Fuel/Blood idk which feature matters more—redirecting threat vs like violating consent/using someone as a tool—and I’m lost how act consequentialism treats those as “everything else equal.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your intuition is a classic split: “minimize deaths” vs “don’t use a person as a tool.” Now pin down what act consequentialism can *say* about that split.

1) When you say “everything else equal,” what things are you *holding fixed* besides the body count? (e.g., pain, fear, future behavior, trust, precedent, probability of success). Which 2–3 factors do you think matter most?

2) If *literally* only the numbers differ (5 die vs 1 dies) and you ignore downstream effects, can act consequentialism treat switch and footbridge differently? Why or why not?

3) So if an act consequentialist wants to explain why footbridge feels worse, what *extra consequences* could they point to that plausibly differ between “flip a switch” and “push a person”? Name one candidate consequence.

4) For Fuel/Blood: what do you think the case is designed to test—(a) pure numbers, (b) consent/rights, or (c) the moral relevance of intending harm vs merely foreseeing it? Which option best fits what confused you?

Reply with your answers to 1–4. Then we’ll decide: are you aiming to defend act consequentialism (by adding those extra consequences) or argue it *gets the cases wrong* (and then you’ll need a non-consequentialist-looking principle for your “right general account”).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll validate the student’s distinction and then guide them to articulate what “everything else equal” must mean for act consequentialism and why it often collapses the switch/footbridge distinction unless additional consequences are introduced. Then I’ll use guided discovery to get them to identify what extra consequences could differ in Fuel/Blood (e.g., incentives, fear, institutional effects, rule compliance) without supplying a finished argument. I’ll keep questions targeted and short, pushing them to propose candidate “other consequences” and to decide whether they want to defend act consequentialism or criticize it and propose a better general account.

exchanges.student: uhh “everything else equal” I guess I’m holding fixed like pain + probability it works, but I can’t tell what else counts (trust? precedent?). And if it’s *literally* just 5 vs 1, then act consequentialism can’t treat switch vs footbridge different, right? For Fuel/Blood I think it’s about consent/rights vs intending harm, but I’m not sure which the case is targeting.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—if it’s *literally* only 5 vs 1, act consequentialism has no room to distinguish switch from footbridge. That’s an important step.

Now you have a fork in the road:

1) If you want to *defend* act consequentialism: you need to say what other consequences aren’t actually “equal.” Which of these do you think is most plausible to include as a real consequence (pick one):
- effects on general fear/psychological security
- effects on trust in others/institutions
- incentives/precedent (people acting differently in the future)
- the risk of abuse/mistakes when “pushing” is permitted

2) If you want to *criticize* act consequentialism: you need to articulate a principle that explains your intuition (“don’t use as a tool,” “need consent,” “rights constrain,” “intending harm matters”). Which principle seems most defensible to you, and why?

For Fuel/Blood, don’t guess the “target” yet—reconstruct it.

3) In one sentence each, what is the *act* you’d have to do in Fuel and in Blood (as you remember it)? And what’s the *one feature* that makes you hesitate most in each (consent? using as means? intending harm? something else)?

Answer 1–3, and we’ll line up: (i) what act consequentialism says, (ii) why it clashes (or not), and (iii) what your “right general account” should include.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll confirm the student’s key inference (AC collapses the distinction under strict equalization) and then guide them to see why philosophers introduce “other consequences” and what counts as a consequence for act consequentialism. Next I’ll steer them to reconstruct what Fuel/Blood are likely probing by asking them to state the action options and what makes them feel wrong, without me supplying the case details or conclusions. I’ll keep it short and require the student to articulate candidate downstream effects or a rights/intent principle to use in their critique/general account.

exchanges.student:
ngl I’m leaning “criticize” bc adding fear/trust feels kinda ad hoc? The principle I *want* is like “don’t kill someone as a means / need consent,” but idk how to say it cleanly.

Also I’m blanking on Fuel/Blood details—I just remember one was like siphoning fuel from someone? and Blood was like taking someone’s blood/organs w/o consent? The hesitation is def the no-consent/using-them part.
exchanges.tutor:
If you’re criticizing, “ad hoc” is a fair worry—but you still need a *sharpened* alternative.

1) When you say “as a means,” what’s your test? In one line: what would make the person’s death/injury a *tool* rather than a side-effect? (Try to state a condition you could apply to switch vs footbridge.)

2) Is *consent* doing the real work for you? Quick probe: if the 1 person on the footbridge explicitly consented to being pushed to save 5, would it flip to permissible for you, or still no? That answer tells you whether your principle is “consent-based” or “means/rights even with consent.”

3) You’re fuzzy on Fuel/Blood—go pull the exact descriptions from Lecture 13 notes (even 2–3 sentences each) and paste them. Without that, you risk arguing against a straw man.

4) Meanwhile, based on your memory: do Fuel/Blood feel more like *harvesting from an innocent* (initiating harm) rather than *redirecting an existing threat*? If yes, how does that map onto your means/consent idea?

Reply with (1) your means-test attempt, (2) your consent answer, and (3) the pasted Fuel/Blood setups when you have them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll keep the student on the assignment by having them articulate a non-consequentialist constraint in their own terms, then pressure-test it with guided counterexamples (consent, means vs side-effect, killing vs letting die) without providing a final principle. Since they’re fuzzy on Fuel/Blood, I’ll ask them to retrieve the exact setups from notes/lecture and, meanwhile, use their rough memory to identify the morally relevant structure. I’ll avoid writing a clean principle for them; instead I’ll ask them to propose necessary/sufficient conditions and refine iteratively.

exchanges.student: ok I’m stuck making a clean “means” test—like how do I say why footbridge is “using as a tool” but switch isn’t? also if the footbridge guy consented, I think it *would* feel more ok, but I’m not 100% sure. I’ll try to find the exact Fuel/Blood text rn and paste it next.
exchanges.tutor:
Try building the “means” test from the *plan’s success condition*.

1) In footbridge, does your plan to save the 5 succeed *because* the person’s body stops the trolley? If the body didn’t stop it, would pushing still save anyone?

2) In switch, does your plan to save the 5 succeed *because* the 1 person is hit, or does it succeed because the trolley is rerouted away from the 5 (with the 1 death as a foreseen result of where the trolley goes)? Put differently: if (impossibly) the side track were empty, would flipping the switch still save the 5?

Now turn those into your own draft criterion:
3) Fill in the blank in your words: “An agent uses someone as a means when ________.” (Aim for one necessary condition, not perfection.)

On consent:
4) If consent makes it “more ok,” what is consent doing—waiving a right, changing what you’re allowed to do, or changing whether it counts as ‘using’? Which of those seems least messy to defend?

Send your answers to 1–4 (even rough). Then we’ll stress-test your draft on switch vs footbridge before we plug in Fuel/Blood.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll help the student operationalize a ‘means’ test by guiding them to compare causal roles in the two trolley variants (what makes the plan succeed). I’ll present structured prompts (fill-in-the-blank style) and ask them to generate the criterion themselves, not provide it. I’ll also use their tentative consent intuition to suggest a direction (consent-sensitive constraint) while warning about edge cases, again via questions. Keep it short and actionable until they paste Fuel/Blood.

exchanges.student: ok so I *think* footbridge works only bc the guy’s body stops the trolley, but switch feels like it works bc u reroute it away from the 5 (even though the 1 dying is kinda predictable). Maybe “using as a means” is when the rescue plan *depends on* the person getting harmed? For consent, I guess it’s like waiving a right, but I’m not sure how to argue that cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
That “depends on the person getting harmed” idea is a strong start—but you need to see if it overgenerates.

1) Test it: in *standard switch*, does saving the 5 “depend on” the 1 being harmed, or only on the track being empty of the 5? How would you phrase the dependence so it fits your intuition?

2) Harder test (important): what about a “loop” variant where diverting sends the trolley onto a loop that would come back to the 5 unless it hits the 1 on the loop and stops. Your dependence test would label that as “using as a means.” Would you then say it’s like footbridge (not OK) or like switch (OK)? Pick one—your answer helps you refine the principle.

3) Consent/right-waiving: what right is being waived—“not to be killed,” “not to be used,” or “control over one’s body”? Which is the most defensible to you in a college-level argument?

4) Where does this leave act consequentialism: would you say (a) it gets these wrong because it ignores rights/means, or (b) it can recover your verdicts by counting long-run consequences? Which are you arguing for?

Reply to 1–4, and paste the Fuel/Blood text when you have it. Also: write ONE rough thesis claim you might defend (don’t polish it)—just to force clarity.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll acknowledge their promising dependency criterion and push them to refine it to avoid over/under-inclusion by testing it against nearby cases (loop trolley, pushing a button that drops a person, organ harvest). I’ll also guide them to sketch how an act consequentialist would respond (deny the constraint or incorporate indirect effects) while keeping the student generating the content. I’ll keep it brief and end with a request to paste Fuel/Blood and to draft their own tentative thesis sentence (not polished).

exchanges.student: ugh the loop variant totally breaks my “depends on harm” thing—I *think* I’d still say it’s more like footbridge (so not ok?), but I’m unsure. For rights, maybe it’s “control over one’s body” or “not to be used,” and I’m leaning (a) act consequentialism misses that, but I can’t phrase a thesis yet.
exchanges.tutor:
The loop case isn’t “breaking” you—it’s revealing what you actually care about.

1) Why do you think loop is closer to footbridge for you? Choose the best explanation:
- the plan requires the person’s body to stop the trolley (means)
- it feels like you’re selecting someone to die (targeting)
- it’s too close to “using” even without physical pushing

2) If you pick “means,” are you willing to bite this bullet: *any* rescue plan that requires an innocent person’s body/harm as the stopper is impermissible, even if it’s just switching tracks? Yes or no?

3) If “control over one’s body” is your right: what does that right rule out in one clause—“others may not ________ even to save more lives”? (Fill the blank.)

4) Thesis template (you fill it): “Act consequentialism says ________ in these cases, but it’s wrong because ________; the right account is that killing one to save five is permissible only when ________.”

Answer 1–4 with rough fragments. Then paste Fuel/Blood when you find it so we can test whether your proposed right/principle handles them too.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll normalize the confusion and use it productively: the loop case is meant to force a commitment about whether “means” is doing the work or whether some other factor (personal force, contact, agent involvement) is. I’ll guide the student to pick which moral feature they want to treat as fundamental and then craft a rough thesis by answering a fill‑in template, without writing it for them. I’ll keep it bite-sized and redirect them to fetch Fuel/Blood details.

exchanges.student: yeah I think loop feels like footbridge bc the plan needs the person’s body to stop it, like you’re “using” them. I guess my rough thesis is act consequentialism says do it whenever 5>1, but it misses a right not to have your body used/killed as a tool even for good outcomes.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid direction, but it’s still too broad unless you draw a boundary.

1) On your view, is *standard switch* permissible or not? You earlier said yes—does your “no using as a tool” thesis still allow it? If yes, what’s the difference in one crisp idea?

2) Does your principle forbid only “using as a tool,” or also forbid *foreseen* killing as a side-effect? (Where do you place switch?)

3) Consent check (since you were leaning that way): if the 1 consents in footbridge/loop, does your right get waived (making it permissible), or is it still impermissible? Pick one.

4) To connect to the assignment: once you paste Fuel/Blood, we’ll ask: do they look more like “tool-use” (impermissible on your account) or more like “side-effect/redirection” (possibly permissible)?

Answer 1–3 now in plain yes/no + one sentence each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll affirm the student’s emerging position and immediately push them to make it precise by specifying permissibility conditions and handling obvious objections (what counts as “using,” what about consent, what about switching that foreseeably kills). I’ll keep it Socratic and require them to supply examples and boundaries. I’ll also keep them aligned with the prompt: apply to Fuel/Blood and trolley cases, and assess whether AC gets them right.

exchanges.student: uhh I *think* standard switch is still ok for me bc the 1’s death is a side-effect of rerouting, not the “stopper” tool, but I’m shaky on that. I’m not trying to forbid all foreseen killing, just the “using their body as the mechanism,” and if the 1 fully consented I’d probably say it becomes permissible (but I’m not totally sure).
exchanges.tutor:
You’re close. Now make the “mechanism vs side-effect” line operational.

1) Criterion attempt: in your own words, is it this? “Someone is used as a tool when their body/harm is part of the causal mechanism that makes the rescue succeed.” Do you accept that, or would you tweak it?

2) Apply it quickly:
- If the side track were empty, flipping the switch still saves the 5: does that support “not using as tool”?
- In footbridge/loop, if the body didn’t stop the trolley, nothing is saved: does that support “using as tool”?
Are you willing to commit to those two tests?

3) Consent: what exactly does consent change in your view—does it (a) remove the rights-violation, or (b) make “using” not wrong, or (c) something else? Pick one.

4) Next step: paste the exact Fuel/Blood descriptions. Then we’ll run your two tests on them and see whether your principle is consistent or needs revision.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll help the student stabilize their distinctions by asking them to state a workable criterion separating side-effect vs mechanism, then test it with minimal variations (empty track, heavy person, button-drop). I’ll also probe their consent commitment and flag that they’ll need to justify why consent changes permissibility. I’ll keep it short and end by requesting Fuel/Blood text to apply the criterion, without providing final conclusions.

exchanges.student: yeah I accept that criterion: “tool” = their body/harm is part of the causal mechanism that makes saving the 5 work. And yeah I can commit to those two tests (empty side track still saves = not tool; if body doesn’t stop it then no one saved = tool). For consent, I think it removes the rights-violation (like they allow it).
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you need to justify *why* “causal mechanism/tool-use” has moral weight (otherwise an act consequentialist will say it’s irrelevant).

1) What’s your best reason that making someone part of the mechanism is especially wrong—does it show disrespect, violate bodily autonomy, treat them as an object, or something else? Pick ONE and say it in 1–2 sentences.

2) How would an act consequentialist push back? What would they say about your criterion—“why should causality matter if outcomes are better”? Give their strongest one-line objection.

3) Your reply (one line): why isn’t that objection decisive?

4) Write a rough 4-bullet outline (no full prose):
- what AC says about kill-1-save-5
- switch/footbridge/loop verdicts under AC
- your principle + consent clause
- how Fuel/Blood fit (once you paste them)

Send 1–4. Then we’ll stress-test the outline for gaps.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll consolidate the student’s progress and guide them to anticipate an act consequentialist rebuttal and strengthen their critique by explaining why their constraint isn’t just intuition. I’ll do this via targeted questions: why causal-mechanism matters morally, how to handle “killing vs letting die,” and how to apply to Fuel/Blood once provided. I’ll keep it bite-sized and ask them to draft (not polished) a brief argument outline in their own words.

deductions.reason: The tutor kept advancing the argument but did not pause to prompt reflection on why the student's new distinctions or method worked, missing a clear chance to reinforce transfer.
The tutor repeatedly bundled several questions and sub-tasks into single turns even when the student said they were confused or lost, making the interaction less bite-sized than it should have been.

overview: Evaluated Transcript 1 of 3 due to the single-conversation scoring schema.
The tutor stayed strongly Socratic, built on the student's developing distinctions, and remained well anchored to the assignment's core philosophical task.
Main weaknesses were communication load from repeated multi-part prompts and limited explicit reflection on why the student's method or revised principle was improving.
